story:
Uncle John bangs open our front door, grinning, saying, “Everything’s gonna be fine.” It’s a hot Saturday in Biloxi, the windows open but the breeze just pushes heat around. I’m nine, sitting with Mary Ann, who’s nine too and lives next door, playing her brother’s Switch. I tell her for the third time we’re moving to Houston because Mama and Donald are getting married. She keeps saying maybe they’ll adopt me. I don’t like that. I live with my mother. Mary Ann’s got a new black tooth today, only it’s “camouflage” she made with the shiny cigarette-foil from her daddy’s pack. She runs in and out, flighty as ever, saying her mama told her getting married was the worst thing she ever did, so her mama won’t come.

Our place gets busy. Uncle John—he’s Donald’s father though everybody calls him “uncle”—keeps hauling in a cooler, ice, and beer. He says the wedding is today. Donald lifts things in a big warehouse and the company wants him to run one in Houston, maybe because he looks like a weightlifter. He took me to a high school football game and says in Houston we’ll go to a real NFL game. Mama wants to sell insurance there because there are millions of people. She hasn’t sold any yet, but her half brother Philip in Jackson sells insurance and buys a new car every two years. Mama says she’s sick and has stayed in bed for two weeks except to eat and go to the bathroom, but she swears she can sell over the phone.

Aunt Peggy Sue brings a big flat pink box I think is a cake and scotch-tapes it shut in the fridge. I try to peek, but Mama’s wheelchair squeaks in, Aunt Alma behind her. Mama’s not in pajamas; she’s in a dress. Weird. She hunts beer in the cooler. I ask if I can have a sip. “Are you fifteen yet?” she says, so no. Uncle Charlie arrives. I set up folding chairs.

Donald stumbles in with roses and little white flowers, wobbly and grinning. Uncle John says, “Let’s get this show on the road.” Mama says she’s too sick to stand. Uncle John says she’s not. Uncle Charlie says Uncle John ain’t a preacher and can’t marry them. Peggy Sue raises her phone, taking pictures, and everybody tells Charlie to sit down. Uncle John pulls two clean papers from his back pocket—printed vows—and reads “Dearly beloved…” and the blah blah blah. He asks each if they take the other. They both say yes. He declares them married. It’s a homemade ceremony, not official, but in our kitchen with roses and family, it counts.

We clap. There’s beer and two bottles of Mountain Dew for me. Aunt Alma cuts big slices of cake. When Donald asks what it says, Aunt Alma tells him it’s “love and joy and best wishes for the newlyweds.”

We move to the living room. I eat cake and wonder what happens now they’re married—am I like Pinocchio now, a real boy? Mary Ann knocks and edges in. Aunt Alma sends me to cut her a slice. In the kitchen, I lift the lid. The sheet cake actually says “Years of Service,” like from somebody’s job party. It makes the day feel even more taped-together, but I sort of love it.

Mary Ann squints at me. “Bobby, are they gonna adopt you? If you go to Texas and they don’t, you’ll be an illegal alien.” That’s wrong, and I tell her to shut up. She grabs two pieces and runs.

Donald finds me. He asks if he looks okay. He does. He says what Mary Ann said is “just bullshit,” that adoption is for people without parents, and I’ve got my mom and now him to be my dad. We’ll be happy in Houston. I can mail Mary Ann a postcard.

I feel happy and shaky, and my face gets wet. I wipe it on my T-shirt, go back to finish my piece of Mama and Daddy’s wedding cake, and hear Uncle John’s voice in my head: Everything’s gonna be fine.
